threats.title: transferToUnoccupiedPlot Missing plotId Update Permanently Bricks Landlord Plots
threats.hypothesis_description: transferToUnoccupiedPlot updates plotOccupied for both old and new plots but never updates toilerState[tokenId].plotId to the new plotId. When the renter subsequently calls unstakeMunchable, it reads stale _toiler.plotId (the old plot) and clears plotOccupied for the already-empty old slot, leaving plotOccupied[landlord][newPlotId] permanently set to occupied=true with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at lines ~155-175 clearly shows plotOccupied updates for both old and new plots, latestTaxRate update, but no assignment to toilerState[tokenId].plotId. In unstakeMunchable (lines ~130-145), plotOccupied[_toiler.landlord][_toiler.plotId] is cleared using the stale plotId. This creates an irreversible state corruption.

proof_of_signal.pattern: plotOccupied updated for old and new plotId but toilerState[tokenId].plotId never assigned newPlotId

preconditions: Renter has a staked Munchable on a landlord's plot
Another unoccupied plot exists on the same landlord
Renter calls transferToUnoccupiedPlot then unstakeMunchable

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing state update: toilerState[tokenId].plotId not updated to new plotId
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After transfer, toilerState.plotId still references old plot. unstakeMunchable clears wrong plotOccupied entry.

analysis_questions: Can this be exploited to grief ALL of a landlord's plots by repeating stake->transfer->unstake?
Is there any admin recovery mechanism for permanently occupied plots?
Does the dirty flag path interact with the stale plotId to create additional issues?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: stake on plot 0, transfer to plot 3, unstake. Verify plot 3 is permanently occupied.
Verify no other code path clears plotOccupied[landlord][newPlotId]
Confirm repeated exploitation can brick all landlord plots

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker stakes on landlord's plot 0, transfers to plot 3, then unstakes. Plot 0 was already cleared during transfer, so unstake clears it again (no-op). Plot 3 remains occupied=true permanently. Repeat for each plot to brick entire landlord capacity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Landlord permanently loses staking capacity and associated tax revenue. All affected plots are bricked with no recovery.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All landlords; any renter can grief any landlord
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without contract upgrade. No admin function to clear plotOccupied.

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers transferToUnoccupiedPlot followed by unstakeMunchable verifying plot state

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction for transfer, second for unstake
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain via permanently occupied plots with no staked token

threats.title: Arithmetic Underflow in Dirty Flag Path Permanently Locks Staked NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: In _farmPlots, when a landlord's plot count decreases below a toiler's plotId, timestamp is set to plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated. If lastUpdated < _toiler.lastToilDate (landlord metadata was last updated before the renter staked), the subtraction (timestamp - _toiler.lastToilDate) underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8.25 checked arithmetic. Since unstakeMunchable uses forceFarmPlots modifier, this revert propagates, making the NFT permanently irretrievable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The dirty flag path at lines ~200-205 sets timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated without validating it is >= _toiler.lastToilDate. The schnibbles calculation at ~220 performs unchecked subtraction. The forceFarmPlots modifier on unstakeMunchable creates an inescapable dependency.
preconditions: Renter staked at time T1
Landlord's plotMetadata.lastUpdated was set at time T0 < T1
Landlord's locked value subsequently decreases reducing plot count below renter's plotId
No intermediate farm call occurred to update lastToilDate

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dirty flag path sets timestamp to potentially stale value; unchecked subtraction follows
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated can be older than toiler.lastToilDate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that timestamp >= _toiler.lastToilDate before subtraction

analysis_questions: What conditions cause plotMetadata.lastUpdated to be older than lastToilDate?
Can a landlord deliberately trigger this to grief renters?
Is there any alternate unstake path that bypasses _farmPlots?
validation_requirements: PoC: Set up scenario where metadata lastUpdated < lastToilDate, landlord reduces plots, verify revert
Verify unstakeMunchable has no bypass for forceFarmPlots
Check if any admin function can rescue locked NFTs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Landlord triggers metadata at T0. Renter stakes at T1 > T0. Landlord unlocks tokens, reducing plot count. Renter tries to farm or unstake -> dirty path sets timestamp=T0, subtraction T0-T1 underflows, revert. NFT is permanently locked.
prerequisites: Landlord whose metadata was updated before renter's stake

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of staked NFTs for affected renters
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any renter whose landlord reduces locked value after staking
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without contract upgrade; no emergency withdrawal function exists

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for dirty flag path with timestamp < lastToilDate

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Landlord needs locked value to create/remove plots
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: stake in one block, landlord unlocks later
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when user attempts unstake and it reverts

threats.title: Reward Formula Systematically Reduces All Schnibbles by 100x
threats.hypothesis_description: The schnibbles calculation formula `uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100)` computes `schnibblesTotal * (1 + finalBonus) / 100`. When finalBonus=0 (no bonus), the result is schnibblesTotal/100 — only 1% of base rewards. The correct formula should be `schnibblesTotal + (schnibblesTotal * finalBonus / 100)` which equals `schnibblesTotal * (100 + finalBonus) / 100`, yielding 100% of base when finalBonus=0.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~220 shows the formula divides the entire expression by 100. With finalBonus=0, the +1 from the original schnibblesTotal means users get 1/100 of expected rewards. This is a demonstrable math error that affects every farming operation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: schnibblesTotal = uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100) — divides base rate by 100

preconditions: Any renter calls farmPlots or triggers farming via modifier
finalBonus is in the expected small integer range (e.g., -20 to +20)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: With finalBonus=0: result = S/100 instead of S. With finalBonus=5: result = S*6/100 instead of S*105/100
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No unit test verifying zero-bonus yields base rate

analysis_questions: Could REALM_BONUSES/RARITY_BONUSES be configured with values ~100 to compensate for this formula?
Are there downstream systems that depend on correct schnibbles accounting?
Is BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE set 100x higher to compensate?
validation_requirements: Compute expected vs actual schnibbles for known bonus values
Check REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES typical ranges from config
Verify BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE is not intentionally inflated 100x

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: All users systematically receive ~1% of their expected rewards. This is not an exploit per se but a code defect causing massive reward underpayment. The bonus multiplier effect is also distorted — a 5% bonus yields 6% of base instead of 105% of base.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All reward accounting is wrong by approximately 100x. Users receive 1% of intended rewards.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All renters and landlords in the protocol
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix formula; retroactive compensation needed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No unit test verifying reward amounts with known inputs

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Users may notice abnormally low rewards

threats.title: Off-by-One in Dirty Flag Check Allows Farming on Non-Existent Plot
threats.hypothesis_description: The dirty flag check in _farmPlots uses `_getNumPlots(landlord) < _toiler.plotId` but should use `<=`. Since plotIds are 0-indexed and _getNumPlots returns count, when numPlots equals plotId (e.g., numPlots=5, plotId=5), the check 5 < 5 evaluates to false. PlotId 5 is invalid when only plots 0-4 exist, yet the renter continues farming at full rate on a non-existent plot.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~200 uses strict less-than comparison. Plot IDs are 0-indexed (enforced in stakeMunchable with plotId < totalPlotsAvail). When landlord's plots reduce to exactly the renter's plotId value, the boundary case is missed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getNumPlots(landlord) < _toiler.plotId uses strict less-than instead of less-than-or-equal

preconditions: Renter staked on plotId N
Landlord's locked value decreases such that _getNumPlots returns exactly N
Renter farms without unstaking

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Boundary plotId continues earning full rewards on non-existent plot

analysis_questions: How often do landlords' plot counts decrease by exactly 1?
Does this produce unbacked reward inflation?
Can this be combined with the timestamp underflow for amplified impact?
validation_requirements: Test with numPlots = plotId to verify dirty flag is not set
Quantify reward overpayment for boundary plot renters
Verify stakeMunchable correctly uses < for bounds check (it does)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Renter stakes on highest available plotId. Landlord slightly reduces locked value. Renter's plotId is now exactly at the boundary but dirty flag never triggers, allowing continued farming on a plot that no longer economically exists.
prerequisites: Landlord reduces locked value to exactly plotId

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate - unbacked reward accrual for boundary renters

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for exact boundary condition where numPlots == plotId

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs naturally when landlords adjust locked value
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect without monitoring plot counts vs staked plotIds

threats.title: Staking Limit Off-by-One Allows 11 NFTs Instead of Intended 10
threats.hypothesis_description: The staking limit check `if (munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10)` allows a user to push an 11th element when length is exactly 10, since 10 > 10 is false. The intended maximum appears to be 10 staked NFTs per account.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~107 checks > 10 instead of >= 10. The push happens after this check, so when length=10, the check passes and length becomes 11.
preconditions: User already has 10 staked Munchables
User has an 11th approved Munchable NFT

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User can stake 11 NFTs when limit should be 10

analysis_questions: Does the uint8 loop iterator in _farmPlots handle 11 staked tokens correctly?
Does the extra staked NFT create any gas issues in farming loops?
Are there any downstream systems that assume max 10?
validation_requirements: Confirm staking 11 NFTs succeeds
Verify _farmPlots loop handles 11 iterations correctly with uint8 counter

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User stakes 11 Munchables instead of the intended limit of 10, gaining a minor advantage in reward generation capacity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minor - one extra staked NFT per user, ~10% more reward capacity than intended
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol fairness affected for all users

threats.test_coverage_gap: No boundary test at exactly 10 staked NFTs

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking munchablesStaked array length

threats.title: Negative Bonus Causes int256-to-uint256 Underflow Minting Near-Infinite Schnibbles
threats.hypothesis_description: If REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES combine to produce finalBonus <= -2, the expression (schnibblesTotal + schnibblesTotal * finalBonus) becomes negative. Division by 100 may still be negative, and casting to uint256 wraps to an astronomically large value (~2^256). Additionally, RARITY_BONUSES is uint8[] but cast via int8, so values 128-255 become negative (-128 to -1), amplifying the risk of negative finalBonus.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~218 performs `uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100)`. With the formula bug (dividing by 100), even finalBonus = -2 makes the numerator = S + S*(-2) = -S, result = -S/100 which is negative. uint256 cast wraps this to ~2^256.
deployment_assumptions: REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES are configured such that their combined value can be <= -2
threats.config_evidence: REALM_BONUSES loaded from configStorage.getSmallIntArray(StorageKey.RealmBonuses) - int16 values could include negatives. RARITY_BONUSES loaded as uint8 but cast to int8 at line ~213, values 128-255 become negative. No deployment config provided to verify actual values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: int256-to-uint256 cast without checking negativity; RARITY_BONUSES uint8->int8 reinterpretation

preconditions: REALM_BONUSES + RARITY_BONUSES sum to <= -2 for some realm/rarity combination
A Munchable NFT exists with that realm/rarity combination
Renter stakes and farms with that NFT on a landlord with the matching snuggeryRealm

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uint256 cast of potentially negative int256 result
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No negativity check before cast; uint8->int8 reinterpretation creates hidden negative values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check: if (result < 0) revert or result = 0

analysis_questions: What are the actual REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES values in production?
Can any realm/rarity combination produce finalBonus <= -2?
Does the /100 formula bug make this more likely by changing the threshold from -100 to -2?
validation_requirements: Retrieve actual REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES from ConfigStorage
Compute all possible finalBonus values across realm and rarity combinations
Test with negative finalBonus to confirm uint256 wrap

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If configuration allows a negative bonus (e.g., cross-realm penalty of -5, rarity bonus uint8=130 -> int8=-126), finalBonus = -131. Calculation: S + S*(-131) = -130S, /100 = -1.3S as int256. Cast to uint256 = ~2^256. Both renter and landlord receive near-infinite schnibbles, destroying the game economy.
prerequisites: Specific realm/rarity combination producing negative finalBonus <= -2

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete destruction of schnibbles economy - near-infinite reward minting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol participants affected by hyperinflation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade and retroactive reward cleanup

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with negative bonus values

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Need specific NFT with triggering attributes

threats.title: Config Key Mismatch in _reconfigure Loads Wrong Values for Critical Parameters
threats.hypothesis_description: _reconfigure loads MIN_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.LockManager, MAX_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.AccountManager, DEFAULT_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.ClaimManager, BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE from StorageKey.MigrationManager, and PRICE_PER_PLOT from StorageKey.NFTOverlord. These StorageKeys are designed for contract addresses, not numeric configuration values. Depending on ConfigStorage implementation, getUint may return 0 or an address cast as uint256, corrupting all economic parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lines ~62-78 show getAddress calls using the same StorageKey enum members (LockManager, AccountManager) that are then reused for getUint calls. This strongly suggests a copy-paste error where the wrong keys are used for numeric parameters.
deployment_assumptions: ConfigStorage.getUint returns meaningful values for address-typed StorageKeys
threats.config_evidence: LandManager.sol lines 62-78: getUint(StorageKey.LockManager) for MIN_TAX_RATE, getUint(StorageKey.AccountManager) for MAX_TAX_RATE, etc. Same keys used for getAddress on lines 52-60. No deployment config or ConfigStorage source provided to verify behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getUint(StorageKey.LockManager) for MIN_TAX_RATE - StorageKey intended for address resolution

preconditions: ConfigStorage stores separate namespaces for address and uint, or getUint returns zero for address-type keys
LandManager is deployed and _reconfigure is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: StorageKey reuse between getAddress and getUint calls
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MIN_TAX_RATE, MAX_TAX_RATE, DEFAULT_TAX_RATE, BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE, PRICE_PER_PLOT all loaded from wrong keys
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation of loaded values (e.g., PRICE_PER_PLOT > 0)

analysis_questions: Does ConfigStorage use separate storage slots for getAddress vs getUint with the same key?
What values does getUint actually return for address-type StorageKeys?
If PRICE_PER_PLOT = 0, does _getNumPlots revert with division by zero?
validation_requirements: Examine ConfigStorage implementation for key-type separation
Test getUint with StorageKey.LockManager on deployed ConfigStorage
Verify if zero PRICE_PER_PLOT causes complete DoS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If PRICE_PER_PLOT loads as 0, _getNumPlots divides by zero, reverting all staking, unstaking, and farming calls. Already-staked NFTs become permanently locked. If ConfigStorage returns address values as uint256, MIN/MAX_TAX_RATE could be huge, making all tax updates revert.
prerequisites: ConfigStorage implementation returns wrong/zero values for mismatched key types

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete contract DoS; all staked NFTs permanently locked; economic parameters corrupted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix key mappings

threats.test_coverage_gap: No integration test verifying correct config values loaded after _reconfigure

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by querying state variables post-deployment

threats.title: Dirty Flag Timestamp Uses Latest Metadata Update Instead of Plot Invalidation Time
threats.hypothesis_description: When the dirty flag triggers in _farmPlots, timestamp is set to plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated. If the landlord's metadata was updated multiple times after the plot became invalid, lastUpdated reflects the most recent update, not the time the plot was first invalidated. This grants the renter rewards for a longer period than they should receive.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~201 sets `timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated`. The comment in the code (lines ~196-199) explicitly acknowledges this edge case but the protocol documentation confirms this is a known accepted risk. However, the impact may be larger than acknowledged if a landlord actively manipulates their metadata update timing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated; code comment acknowledges edge case of multiple updates

preconditions: Renter staked on a plot that becomes invalid
Landlord's metadata is updated after the plot became invalid (e.g., via new lock/unlock)
Renter has not farmed between these events

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: lastUpdated reflects latest metadata update, not first plot invalidation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Renter earns rewards for period between plot invalidation and latest metadata update
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tracking of when specific plot was first invalidated

analysis_questions: Can a renter collude with a landlord to maximize this overpayment?
What triggers updatePlotMetadata calls that advance lastUpdated?
How large can the reward overpayment realistically be?
validation_requirements: Construct timeline: plot invalidated at day 10, metadata updated day 25, renter farms day 30
Quantify excess rewards: 25 days instead of 10 days of accrual
Verify the protocol documentation acknowledges this as accepted risk

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Renter stakes on plotId 5, doesn't farm for 30 days. Day 10: landlord's plots reduce to 5. Day 25: landlord locks new tokens, triggering metadata update (lastUpdated = day 25). Day 30: renter farms, dirty triggers with timestamp = day 25. Renter gets 25 days of rewards instead of 10.
prerequisites: Landlord metadata updated after plot invalidation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate overpayment of schnibbles rewards; magnitude depends on time gap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Landlords indirectly (paying tax on inflated rewards)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires architectural change to track per-plot invalidation timestamps

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multiple metadata updates between farm calls

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect without comparing plot invalidation time vs reward period

threats.title: Multiple Staked Tokens With Same Landlord May Lose Landlord Schnibbles
threats.hypothesis_description: In _farmPlots, each loop iteration fetches landlordMetadata fresh via _getMainAccountRequireRegistered(landlord) and calls accountManager.updatePlayer(landlord, landlordMetadata) at the end. If a renter has multiple tokens staked with the same landlord, the second iteration fetches landlordMetadata again. If accountManager.getPlayer does not immediately reflect the updatePlayer from iteration 1, the second iteration's landlordMetadata starts from the pre-update state, and the updatePlayer overwrites iteration 1's schnibbles addition.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lines ~207 and ~230-231 show per-iteration fetch and update of landlordMetadata. If two tokens share a landlord, the pattern is: fetch(100)->add(10)->write(110)->fetch(?)->add(15)->write(?). If fetch returns 100 instead of 110, the final write is 115 instead of 125, losing 10 schnibbles.

proof_of_signal.pattern: landlordMetadata fetched and written per-iteration; stale read possible if accountManager caches

preconditions: Renter has 2+ tokens staked with the same landlord
accountManager.getPlayer does not immediately reflect updatePlayer writes within the same transaction

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-iteration fetch+update of landlord metadata without local accumulation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Stale read pattern if accountManager has any caching or storage delay
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No local landlord metadata cache; should accumulate locally like renterMetadata

analysis_questions: Does accountManager.updatePlayer immediately persist changes visible to getPlayer?
Is accountManager.getPlayer a direct storage read or does it use memory caching?
How often do renters stake multiple tokens with the same landlord?
validation_requirements: Examine accountManager.updatePlayer and getPlayer implementation
Test: stake 2 tokens with same landlord, farm, verify landlord receives both schnibble amounts
Compare landlord schnibbles with expected sum

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A renter stakes 3 tokens with the same landlord. On farming, only the last iteration's landlord schnibbles are preserved because each iteration overwrites with a fresh (stale) copy. The landlord loses schnibbles from the first 2 iterations.
prerequisites: Multiple tokens staked with same landlord

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Landlords lose a portion of their tax revenue (all but last iteration's schnibbles)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Landlords with multiple renters or renters with multiple tokens on same landlord
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to accumulate landlord metadata locally like renter metadata

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multiple tokens staked with same landlord in single farm call

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing expected vs actual landlord schnibbles after farming

threats.title: Tax Rate > 1e18 Causes Renter Schnibbles Underflow Locking NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: If MAX_TAX_RATE is misconfigured (due to the config key mismatch) to a value > 1e18, a landlord could set latestTaxRate > 1e18. In _farmPlots, schnibblesLandlord = (schnibblesTotal * latestTaxRate) / 1e18 would exceed schnibblesTotal. The subsequent subtraction renterMetadata.unfedSchnibbles += (schnibblesTotal - schnibblesLandlord) would underflow and revert in Solidity 0.8.25, blocking farming and unstaking.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~225 computes schnibblesLandlord which could exceed schnibblesTotal if latestTaxRate > 1e18. Line ~229 subtracts without checking. The forceFarmPlots modifier on unstakeMunchable propagates this revert.
deployment_assumptions: MAX_TAX_RATE loaded from wrong StorageKey could result in value > 1e18
threats.config_evidence: MAX_TAX_RATE loaded from StorageKey.AccountManager (line ~70), which may return an address-as-uint256, potentially a very large value. No validation that MAX_TAX_RATE <= 1e18.

proof_of_signal.pattern: schnibblesLandlord = (schnibblesTotal * latestTaxRate) / 1e18; renter gets (schnibblesTotal - schnibblesLandlord) — underflows if tax > 100%

preconditions: MAX_TAX_RATE is set to > 1e18 (due to config mismatch or admin error)
Landlord sets tax rate to a value > 1e18
Renter farms or attempts to unstake

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No check that schnibblesLandlord <= schnibblesTotal before subtraction
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Underflow in checked arithmetic causes revert; forceFarmPlots prevents unstaking
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cap on latestTaxRate at 1e18; no safe subtraction

analysis_questions: What is the actual MAX_TAX_RATE value loaded from ConfigStorage?
Can a landlord set tax rate > 1e18 with current bounds?
Would this permanently lock all NFTs staked with that landlord?
validation_requirements: Check actual MAX_TAX_RATE value from ConfigStorage
Test with latestTaxRate = 2e18, verify farming reverts
Confirm unstaking is blocked when farming reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Config misconfiguration allows MAX_TAX_RATE > 1e18. Landlord sets tax rate to 1.5e18. When renters farm, schnibblesLandlord = 1.5 * schnibblesTotal, exceeding schnibblesTotal. Subtraction underflows, reverting. All renters' NFTs on that landlord are locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent NFT lock for all renters staked with the malicious/misconfigured landlord
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All renters on affected landlord's plots
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires config fix to reduce MAX_TAX_RATE, then landlord must reduce rate

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for tax rate > 1e18 edge case

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction to set rate; victims affected on next farm
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when users report inability to unstake

threats.title: Landlord Tax Rate Bait-and-Switch Exploits Delayed Farming
threats.hypothesis_description: updateTaxRate does not trigger farming for existing stakers before changing the rate. While toilerState.latestTaxRate provides protection for the current accrued period, the new rate is immediately stored and applied when toilerState.latestTaxRate is updated during the next farm call. A landlord can attract stakers with a low rate, then raise it to MAX_TAX_RATE. The renters' next farming period uses the high rate with no retroactive protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: updateTaxRate (line ~86-94) directly modifies plotMetadata[landlord].currentTaxRate without calling _farmPlots for stakers. In _farmPlots (line ~228), toilerState[tokenId].latestTaxRate is updated to the new rate for the next period.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Tax rate updated directly without farming existing stakers; no forceFarmPlots call

preconditions: Landlord has stakers on their plots
Landlord changes tax rate
Renters do not immediately farm after the rate change

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No farming trigger before tax rate change
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Next farming period immediately uses new rate; renters cannot retroactively lock in the old rate for the current period
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call _farmPlots for all affected stakers before rate change

analysis_questions: How quickly can renters react to a tax rate change?
Is there an event emitted that off-chain monitors could detect?
Could a landlord oscillate rates to systematically extract maximum tax?
validation_requirements: Model: renter stakes at 5% rate, landlord raises to 50%, renter farms days later
Quantify the economic loss to the renter from the rate change
Verify TaxRateChanged event is emitted for off-chain detection

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Landlord sets tax rate to 1% to attract stakers. After multiple users stake, landlord raises to MAX_TAX_RATE. The current period uses the old latestTaxRate (1%), but all subsequent farming accrues at MAX. Renters must notice, farm immediately, then unstake to stop losses.
prerequisites: Ability to change tax rate within bounds

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate - renters lose a portion of rewards to elevated tax for the period between rate change and their next farm/unstake
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All renters staked with the manipulative landlord
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Renters can unstake; no permanent loss but opportunity cost and trust damage

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for tax rate change impact on existing stakers

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Landlord needs locked value for plots
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: set low rate, wait for stakers, raise rate
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: TaxRateChanged event emitted; off-chain monitoring possible
